result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story begins—before the first sentence, before the alphabet arranges itself into meaning. It’s a quiet, luminous pocket of time, like the hush in a theater when the houselights fade but the curtain hasn’t yet risen. In that hush, everything is possible and nothing is obligated. The characters haven’t been assigned their wounds yet; the plot hasn’t chosen which yearning it will use as engine. There is only the raw, breathing void, and somewhere inside it a pulse that asks, “What if?”

Years ago I used to keep a notebook of abandoned first lines. I’d wake at 3 a.m. with some luminous fragment—“The moon that night was a cancelled stamp”—and scribble it down, only to discover by morning that the moon had changed its mind. The line would feel suddenly counterfeit, a backstage pass to a concert that had already ended. I kept the notebook anyway; I liked the archaeology of it, the strata of handwriting getting smaller and more desperate as the pages marched on. Sometimes I’d flip back and find a sentence that looked like it had been written by someone I hadn’t become yet. I’d read it and feel a strange tenderness for the insomniac I used to be, coaxing fireflies into jars without realizing they needed air.

We talk so much about finishing things—manuscripts, degrees, renovations, grief—but I’m enchanted by the etiquette of beginning. The way a seed splits underground before anyone congratulates it. The way a violinist lifts the bow a fraction of an inch before the note decides how it will tremble. My neighbor’s five-year-old daughter knocks on my door every Saturday with a fistful of dandelions and the same breathless announcement: “I’m a DIFFERENT princess today.” She hasn’t learned that identities are supposed to solidify. She thinks transformation is the default setting of the world. I let her in, give her a glass of water, and watch her spin in my kitchen until the dandelion fluff drifts like tiny parachutes exploring peaceful occupations.

The truth is, I’ve grown suspicious of conclusions. They’re useful for postage and mathematics, but they flatten the dimpling surface of lived time. I prefer the hanging colon, the door left ajar, the rumor of another room behind the bookcase. Somewhere in that room is a table where all my unlived lives are playing poker with one another, betting pocket lint and bus transfers, occasionally slipping a wild card into the deck that looks suspiciously like the face I see in the mirror when I remember to look up.

Last week I found a cicada shell clinging to the mailbox—amber translucence, legs curved like aerial parentheses. It was so light it almost wasn’t there. I cupped it in my palm anyway, felt the ghost of a buzz against my lifeline. The creature that stepped out of that armor is long gone, already singing in the treetops, already forgetting the precise shape of the body it left behind. I keep the shell on my desk now, a reminder that molting is not loss but un-occupation, the way darkness isn’t the absence of light but the presence of a different kind of distance.

Tonight the sky is the color of a bruised metaphor, and thunder mutters in the next county like someone rehearsing an argument they’ll never win. I open all the windows, let the storm’s unfinished sentence wander through the house. It tastes of iron, wet earth, the small metallic fear that lives at the back of applause. Somewhere inside it I hear the hush again—curious, uncontracted, thrumming with first lines that have not yet decided whether they will be elegy or invitation. I lift my hands to the keyboard the way a violinist lifts the bow: a fraction of an inch, a breath, a question mark wearing the costume of silence.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
